elements.subsection-2411-005: Why do not we detect TOI-396 c in the RV time series?
elements.sent-2411-126: Magnetic activity combined with stellar rotation induces RV variations that can hide, affect, or even mimic planetary signals \citep[e.g.][]{Queloz2001,Hatzes2010,Dumusque2011a,Haywood2014,suarezMascareno2017,Gandolfi2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: A possible explanation for the non-detection of the Doppler reflex motion induced by TOI-396\,c is that stellar activity destructively interferes with the Keplerian signal of the planet.
elements.sent-2411-128: This may happen if the star has a rotation period comparable to the orbital period of the planet \citep[e.g.][]{vanderburg2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Disentangling the planetary signal from stellar activity and retrieving the Doppler motion induced by the orbiting planet would then be challenging \citep[e.g.][]{dragomir2012,kossakowski2022 .}

Left panels: The red curves in the first four panels mark the quadratic trends and sine functions as obtained from the best fit to the most significant peaks (false alarm probability FAP\,$<$\,0.1\%) identified in the corresponding GLS periodograms. Right panels: The vertical dashed blue lines mark the orbital frequencies of the tree transiting planets. The yellow area encompasses the peaks likely due to stellar rotation. The horizontal dashed red lines mark the 0.1\% false alarm probability.} \label{fig:gls-activity-SN}

elements.sent-2411-132: We assessed the significance of the peaks detected in the power spectra by estimating their false alarm probability (FAP), i.e., the probability that noise could produce a peak with power higher than the one we found in the time series.
elements.sent-2411-133: To account for the possible presence of non-Gaussian noise in the data, we estimated the FAP using the bootstrap randomisation method \citep[see, e.g.,][]{Murdoch1993,Kuerster1997,Hatzes2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Briefly, we computed the GLS periodogram of 10$^5$ mock time series obtained by randomly shuffling the data points along with their error bars, while keeping the timestamps fixed.
elements.sent-2411-135: We defined the FAP as the fraction of those mock periodograms whose highest power exceeds the power of the real data at any frequency.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the present work, we considered a peak to be significant if its false alarm probability is FAP\,$<$\,0.1\,\%.

elements.section-2411-006: Joint RV and TTV dynamical analysis
elements.sent-2411-177: As mentioned, the anti-correlation pattern of the observed TTVs (see Fig.~\ref{fig:TTVbc ) is a typical signal of the dynamical interaction between TOI-396\,b and c.}
elements.sent-2411-178: However, data in our hands are not enough to currently derive meaningful planetary masses from the TTVs.
elements.sent-2411-179: Indeed, the photometric observations are clustered in two groups (about two years apart), which results in a partial coverage of the curvature of the TTVs.
elements.sent-2411-180: This prevents us from accurately mapping the full phases and super-periods of the TTV signals, hence a dynamical fit onto the transit times would lead to a high fraction of low-amplitude TTVs with short super-period or to a low fraction of high-amplitude long-period TTV signals.
elements.sent-2411-181: Despite this, we attempted to run a dynamical joint fit of the TTV and RV data set with \trades{}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/lucaborsato/trades \citep{Borsato2014A A...571A..38B,Borsato2019MNRAS.484.3233B,Borsato2021MNRAS.506.3810B , a Fortran-python code developed to model TTVs and RVs simultaneously along with N-body integration.}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: We have taken and fixed the stellar mass and radius values from Table~\ref{tab:star .}
elements.sent-2411-183: We further fixed the orbital inclinations $i$ to the values in Table~\ref{tab:planets and the longitude of the ascending nodes to $\Omega=180^{\circ}$\footnote{ We assumed the same reference system of \citet{Winn2010 and \citet{Borsato2014A A...571A..38B and later used in \citet{Borsato2019MNRAS.484.3233B,Borsato2021MNRAS.506.3810B,Borsato2024A A...689A..52B,Nascimbeni2023A A...673A..42N,nascimbeni2024 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: We fitted the planetary masses scaled by the stellar mass ($M_\mathrm{b,c,d /M_\star$), the orbital periods ($P$), the eccentricities ($e$) and the arguments of the pericentre ($\omega$) in the form ($\sqrt{e}\cos\omega$, $\sqrt{e}\sin\omega$), and the mean longitudes ($\lambda$\footnote{The mean longitude is defined as $\lambda =\omega + \Omega + \mathcal{M}$, where $\omega$ is the argument of the pericentre, $\Omega$ is the longitude of the ascending node, and $\mathcal{M}$ is the mean anomaly.. ).}}
elements.sent-2411-185: We also fitted for an RV offset ($\gamma_\mathrm{RV}$) and for an RV jitter term ($\sigma_\mathrm{jitter}$) by adopting $\log_{2}\sigma_\mathrm{jitter}$ as step parameter, although we used the detrended RV data set as derived from Section~\ref{sec:TTV , where a jitter term was already included in the RV errors.}
elements.sent-2411-186: All fitting parameters were subject to uniform priors that account for their respective physical boundaries (see Table~\ref{tab:trades_parameters ); for the eccentricities we applied a log-penalty ($\log\mathrm{p _e$) based on the half-Gaussian ($e=0, \sigma_e = 0.083$) from \citet{VanEylen2019AJ....157...61V .}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: The reference time for the dynamical integration of the orbital parameters was set at $T_\mathrm{ref = 2\,458\,379\,\mathrm{BJD_{TDB }$, that is before all available observations.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: We combined the quasi-global differential evolution \citep{Storn1997 optimisation algorithm implemented in \pyde{ \citep{Parviainen2016 with the Affine Invariant MCMC Ensemble sampler \citep{AffineInvariantGoodmanWeare2010 \emcee{ implemented by \citet{DFM2019JOSS....4.1864F,Foreman2013 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: We first ran \pyde{ and evolved 68 different initial configurations of parameter sets for $50\,000$ generations (number of steps for which each parameter is evolved).}
elements.sent-2411-190: To perform the dynamical analysis in an MCMC fashion, we then ran \emcee{ , assuming as starting point the outcome obtained with \pyde.}
elements.sent-2411-191: We set up 68 chains for $600\,000$ steps each.
elements.sent-2411-192: To sample the parameter space efficiently, we mixed the DEMove() and DESnookerMove() differential evolution moves\footnote{See \emcee{ documentation: \url{https://emcee.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorials/moves/ in the proportion 80\%--20\% \citep{DENelson2014ApJS..210...11N,terBraak2008 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: We repeated the sequence \pyde+\emcee{ twice, with different seeds for the random number generator.}
elements.sent-2411-194: The chains reached convergence according to visual inspection and statistical indicators, such as the Gelman-Rubin $\hat{R}$, the Geweke's statistic \citep{geweke1991 , and the auto-correlation function\footnote{See e.g. \citet{AffineInvariantGoodmanWeare2010 and the \emcee{ documentation at \url{https://emcee.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorials/autocorr/\#autocorr .. .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: For both runs, we applied a conservative thinning factor of 100 and discarded the first $50\%$ of the chains (burn-in).
elements.sent-2411-196: For each run, we derived the reference outcome (hereinafter also referred to as best-fit), as the maximum-a-posteriori (MAP) parameters' set, that is the set of parameters that maximises the log-probability\footnote{ The log-probability is given by $\log\mathcal{P = \log\mathcal{L + \log\mathrm{p _e$, where $\log\mathcal{L}$ is the log-likelihood and $\log\mathrm{p _e$ is the log-penalty of the eccentricities.. .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: The uncertainty of each parameter is quantified by the high-density interval (HDI) at 68.27\%\footnote{ An HDI at 68.27\% is the equivalent of the $15.87^\mathrm{th -84.14^\mathrm{th}$ percentile range of a Normal distribution that provides the 1$\sigma$ standard confidence interval.}}
